speaker: interviewer
text: The date is April 5, 1995. Survivor Flora Carasso Mihael. Interviewer is Chana Meier. We're in New York. And interview will be conducted in English. My name is Chana Meier, C-H-A-N-A M-E-I-E-R. And we are here today taping Mrs. Carasso Mihael's interview for the Survivors of the Shoah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Mrs. Mihael. And thank you so much for joining us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Mihael, can you please state your full name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, it's Flora Carasso Mihael. F-L-O-R-A C-A-R-A-- double-S-O M-I-H-A-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Thessaloniki, Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us a little about Thessaloniki, Greece?

speaker: interviewee
text: As it is today or--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born. What you remember before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a a very Jewish city. Were 56,000 Jews living in Thessaloniki. Very close-- the families were very, very close and united. And Jews were in Salonika, because we called it Salonika, also Thessaloniki-- were there before the Greeks gave it to Greece, to Thessaloniki. Until 1912, it was, as we can say, a Jewish city. 1912, the Greek army liberated the city from the Turks because the Turks had conquered Thessaloniki. And the Jews started to strive on with their lives and build a healthy and strong community until the invasion of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born in Thessaloniki?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your family like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was a large family. I remember living always with my aunts, uncles, grandmother, not having many friends outside of the family. It was always-- the houses were built like villas, one near the other, and always playing and feeling the love and care of the family. Not the friends, all family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Rene and my mother's Alien.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was representing a factory from Athens. He used to send the products they use-- they used to produce soap, combs, and glue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although you were very young in the years prior to the war, do you have any memory of your family life at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, my family life changed. We-- our houses were requisition-- requisitioned by the Germans. I remember very well a German officer that lived in our house. And I had the typhus then. And when he learned from my mother, who spoke a little bit of German, that I had typhus, he washed his hands with alcohol and left. And went he got drunk, he used to tell my mother, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means that all the Jews will be shot. And I remember the family said, don't worry. Don't listen to him. He's drunk. Nobody could believe. And later on, my-- my grandmother crying, my mother also. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse-- excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she-- she used to tell me, Mother, don't worry about me. We are going to work. We're going to peel potatoes. And we come back. Because the-- the news were that the Jews would be sent to Madagascar to make a new state there, a Jewish state. And they had also to pay their tickets, their train tickets. But only my father, my mother, and me, and one of my aunts with her husband been were not in this plan because we had the Spanish and the Italian nationality. And since the Italians were fighting with the Germans until a certain date, and the German-- and Spain with Franco was neutral, so we were free to walk on the streets. We were not-- we had not to wear our yellow stars. But all our family, my aunts, uncles, cousins, were-- had to wear their yellow stars until they were brought in a ghetto that was called Baron Hirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before this happened, until 1942, did you go to school at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to grammar school. And sadly, I remember that my mother wouldn't let me go in a-- to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember if I was not allowed to go. To tell you the truth, they didn't tell me. It was a-- I was seven then. I stopped going to school until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school or a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- no, it was not a Jewish school. It was a-- a Greek private school, one of the-- the best for girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you learn any other languages at home besides Greece?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, my parents and all my family spoke Ladino and French. Greek was their third language, which they didn't learn. Until my parents died, they made a lot of mistakes when they spoke in Greek. Their-- their language was Ladino and French because when they went to school, there were no Greek schools. There were only French schools. After 1912, there were schools. Already, my parents were grown-- were grownups.

speaker: interviewer
text: And both your parents were from Thessaloniki--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: --originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of them, all the family, all the family. But we came from Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community life before the war like? Did you go to the synagogues on holidays? Did you have Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of Jewish organizations. There were orphanages. There were-- there was [NON-ENGLISH] where poor people would go out there for soup. There was [NON-ENGLISH] It was a kind of [? vigil ?] before the war where the ladies used to knit and embroider and then sell all of these items in bazaars. And with the money, they would help younger Jewish girls to-- and send them to farm schools in-- in Palestine then. And there was the Maccabi also. Many Jewish schools, many Jewish schools. The first printing was done by a Jew in Thessaloniki. We had a lot of new Greek newspapers written in Ladino, in French, and in Greek. There were a lot of doctors, lawyers, businessman, intellectuals, artists, [INAUDIBLE]. And it was an active Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Zionistic community? Was it very focused on Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I can't tell you because I was very young, perhaps, to understand was-- was-- what was Palestine and Zionism. But after the war, a lot of survivors left for Palestine. And my father was a very good Zionist. And living with him, he gave me-- he transmitted his love for Israel. That's why I'm-- I try to work and I love Israel very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, before the war, getting together with your families and having celebrations together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, no. Before the war, no. It was not a religious family. It-- mine was a traditional family. I don't remember myself before the war going to a synagogue. I don't remember. Because-- perhaps my mother took me. But I don't remember. After the war, of course, I didn't miss the holidays. Not on Shabbat. We didn't light candles at home on Shabbat. But I went on Yom Ha'atzmaut, Yom Kippur, and all the other holidays, always until I do until today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at the outbreak of the war, how was your family affected in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, my father didn't lose his job. There was a lot of chit-chat in the family how they were going to save their-- their houses, their businesses, and not speaking about hiding, not at all. They couldn't imagine what Germans' idea was about to the Final Solution. They couldn't imagine that they were going to Germany or to Poland and they wouldn't come back. And even though there were Greek people that would come and tell the young people of the family, come with us to the fight in the mountains, they wouldn't they their parents. The Jewish family was very, very, very close. And I think that that's why many, many young people died because they were-- they wouldn't let their parents, leave-- leave alone. They said, we're going to Germany. We work and come back. Nobody had come back and tell us-- and I don't know if he had-- if someone had come back, if people could believe what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Greek people in your city helpful at this point? Were they aware of something and tried to alert you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- our community was aware-- aware, because in July-- 7th of July, 1943, all the men from 16 were gathered in the square in Salonika, which is called until today liberation, Liberty Square. And all the men were there. They-- they-- they were kept under a burning sun for hours and hours. Many fainted. And then they took the Jewish to labor camps outside Thessaloniki. They knew, of course, that something was wrong. We had a Rabbi [? Koretz, ?] who was not Greek. He was from Austria. And I don't know how he-- his attitude during the war is ambiguous. It's not very clean. And he tried to persuade Jews to stay put, not to leave, that everything would be all right. The Germans would treat them fine. And they would go to Poland and come back and find everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were very few Jews that ran away, that went to a mountain or went to-- not to Israel. Where it was? They-- they left Greece. They-- they left Salonika for Athens and for the islands also because Jews were not only in Salonika. The were in Athens, in the islands also. And now, there are no communities anymore. At what point after 1939 was your family affected? What-- did your father lose his job at a certain point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father didn't lose his job. He kept his job until we fled to Athens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a ghetto in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Athens, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Thessaloniki.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Thessaloniki, there was a ghetto, Baron Hirsch. It was called Baron Hirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be '43. Then the Jews were all around up there. They had to write down their names on the list of the community. And they were all sent in Baron Hirsch. And that-- that was a ghetto near the railway station. And from there, the Germans took a voice. And they started sending them to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have never visited the ghetto. It was not possible for outsiders to go into the ghetto. It must've been bigger now. When I passed from there, my husband used to tell me that was the ghetto. It's full of houses and buildings. You can see nothing. There isn't even the plaque telling that, you know, in 1943, Jews suffered here. No, nothing. It's just buildings and the railway station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who had to go to the ghetto? And who has exempt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exempt were only Jews with foreign nationality and the-- the community members, the council.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain a Spanish passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: We it before the war, since my great-grandparents came from Spain. But we had it before the war. It was legal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family-- the rest of your family also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just my father, my father, my mother, and me, nobody else in the family. I don't know how. But we were the only ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to stay in your own house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our own house. We moved several times with other families because you were not allowed to have an big apartment for yourself. And I remember, we moved with other-- with two other families, friends of my parents. We moved together.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was they started taking Jews away. It must be about a year before we fled to Athens. Must be about 1943. But I-- I remember leaving freely, walking on the street with my parents, always with my parents, always. I was free. I didn't feel that there was restricted in anything. Perhaps I was a child also and didn't feel it. I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear the adults talking about different plans and plans of escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plans of escape, they wouldn't talk in front of the children. Talking about what-- what would become of their belongings, yes, I remember. They-- they took these, the carpets in one warehouse. For me, it was like a story. I couldn't understand what they were talking about. I didn't miss the carpets. I didn't miss the-- the furniture. Of course, I missed my grandmother and my aunts and uncles and especially one cousin. This I felt very well. But I stayed a child. Since I was near my parents, nothing could hurt me. That's what I believed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you moved with these other families, to what type of apartment did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a nice apartment in the center of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to take your belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were able to take our belongings as much as we could because my father had already given a lot of our belongings to Greek families. Or he would hid-- hide them in warehouses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father gave them to Greek families by choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes by choice, sometimes no. Sometimes no. I remember very well, it happens that I was there because my father didn't tell-- I was there. And I remember someone coming at home. It was a table, a dinner table that I have it. I have it at home. It's very precious to me, for me. And someone telling my father, you give it to me. I'll keep it during the war. And if you come back, I'll give it back to you. And he did. My father knew where it was. And he went and took the table. And I have it at home. Some didn't. But Greeks help a lot, especially young people take them to-- onto the mountains for the resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there some Greeks that hid Jews during the war as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: There must have been. Well, we had traitors in our community. It's not some-- they tell me that you shouldn't say that. Why? We had traitor Jews, traitors in our community. We had people that denounced other Jews. And I don't know how they felt because perhaps it was for their survival. And people-- these people-- those people came back from the camp. And they were just and sentenced in Salonika. And two were shot after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews who were traitors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In 56,000, you can find one or two or three or four.

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- at this point, when you were with other families, did you know these families? Or were you just put together when you moved to a smaller apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew them because they were very, very good friends of my father. I knew them because my mother used to take me on a visit there. And I spent many evenings with them. So I knew them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did normal life continue more or less at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal life with a lot of sadness, with a lot of fear, of course, with not [? knowing ?] what would become of us, [? we're ?] [? not about to get ?] tomorrow, and a lot of privation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, what did you-- what was going on in your head? You had stopped going to school at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've stopped school. I don't remember asking why. I was very, very attached and sad when I saw my cousin leave. He was my dear cousin. And that I felt it. And I feel it even today. And losing, of course, all-- all the aunts that loved me because I was the only girl in the family. That was something. And they didn't have many children. There were two boys and a girl. And my father tried to save my cousin, who was only six months old. He went to the Spanish consulate and tried to persuade them to take the child as his own. But he didn't succeed. They wouldn't let him. I remember all this fuss and all this talking, my father running and trying to save this baby. His name was David. He didn't succeed. He would have grown up and perhaps he wouldn't have even known that he was not my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my father had an employee who unfortunately collaborated. And the day-- one day, he sees my father-- he meets my father on the street and tells him, Rene, are you still leaving Salonika? And I remember my father coming home and said, Alien, we have to leave because he recognized my father. And since he knew that he was not very clean, and he had collaborated with the Germans, he got frightened. I don't-- I don't know how, but he succeeded in-- in fake-- having false identity cards as Christian Greeks. My name was still Flora then. The last name was [? Salto. ?] I don't know how he chose-- chose this name. And we took the [? tradotta. ?] The [? tradotta ?] was a train that took Italian soldiers to Athens. And we went to Athens with these train with false identity card-- papers. And we were received very warmly in a Greek Christian home. It was my father's superior from the factory he used to work to. And his grandson was decorated last year in June because our community decorated and made this special event for the Greeks that helped the Jews. And-- and he came. And he was decorated for the-- what his grandfather saved us. I mean, we were on the street. We didn't have where to live. We didn't have any money. And he took us in his house, the three of us. He even found an apartment where we went to live. And from there, we went to-- were sent to-- to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you manage to get on this train with all the Italian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: With our false identity cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in fear that people would recognize you as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father, yes. I didn't feel anything. It was for a trip for me. Perhaps it was the first trip for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take any belongings with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our belongings were some suitcases. My mother tried to put inside the satchel, especially food. And the-- I remember that all the children and myself used to wear this back-- the bags you use now. Everybody wears these bags. They put it on the shoulder. There were-- there was water and food and some cloth. And of course, I didn't know that. But my mother had sewn a ring with a diamond on my shoulder bag-- bag. I had a red coat. And she had sewn there a ring because you didn't know after the war or doing the war, you didn't have to sell jewelry to save yourself. I didn't know that until much, much later. Perhaps when I was engaged, because she gave me this ring. And she said that it-- I had it on me even in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Athens, did you know the contact? Your father knew he was going to go to his supervisor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He just called them from the railway station. We didn't have anybody. We didn't have any relatives in Athens. So we had to phone call a name. His name was Vasser Condira. I remember this family very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: He called. And he said, Mr. Vasser, I'm here. Where are you? At the train station. I'm with that Alien and Flora. And what are you doing? I don't have where to go. Can-- do you know a hotel? No, you come home. And that picture that is in front of me is an open door with all of the family standing outside waiting for us. And did you live as Christians while you lived in Athens?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no as Jews, as Jews. In Athens, it was not the Thessaloniki. The Jews in Athens were few. And the restrictions were not the same as in Thessaloniki. They didn't have any ghetto. No, we were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just used the false papers to get across to Athens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we used them in Athens also. But I remember going on promenade with my parents and going to the market. But they tried not to speak Greek very much because they had-- their pronunciation, if Jews opened their-- their mouth, you would understand they were not Greeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents look like Jews or was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no noticeable--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in this apartment in Athens?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that house, we're about one month. And then they found a-- another apartment near their home so they could be in touch. But all day, we passed it with them. All my father's questions and troubles, even our jewelry, Mr. Condira put it in a vase with cotton. And then he took two tiles from the-- his garden, sent all the family away, and he put the jewelry there. And after the war, I have the picture that he takes off the tiles. And he gave the vase with the jewelry to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were great people.
